title: YOLO in Practice
concept: YOLO's extreme speed makes it the industry standard for real-time applications on edge hardware.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What are the three main components of the YOLO loss function?

options.text: Localization loss, confidence loss, and classification loss
Anchor loss, resolution loss, and speed loss
Grid loss, convolution loss, and sorting loss

exercises.explain: Correct! YOLO loss combines localization, confidence, and classification errors.

cards.title: Speed is Safety
cards.body: In real-world applications like self-driving cars or warehouse robots, a model that takes 2 seconds to detect an obstacle is useless. Speed is safety! Let's explore how YOLO's speed is used in practice.

cards.title: Real-Time Object Detection
cards.body: Real-Time Object Detection means processing video frames at the same speed they are captured (usually 30+ frames per second). YOLO's single-stage architecture enables real-time performance on edge devices.

cards.title: Flipbook Animation
cards.body: A video is just a fast sequence of photos. If a model detects objects in a single photo in 15 milliseconds, it can easily keep up with the 'flipbook' of video frames, making detection look fluid and continuous.

cards.title: Onboard Drone Tracking
cards.body: A drone flying through a forest uses a lightweight YOLO model (like YOLO-Tiny) on an onboard mobile chip to detect and avoid branches in real-time without needing a internet connection.

cards.title: Do We Always Need GPUs?
cards.body: You might think YOLO requires an expensive server GPU to run. While training does, optimized tiny YOLO models can run inference on simple edge devices like a Raspberry Pi, mobile phones, or smart cameras.

cards.title: Cloud vs. Edge Inference
cards.body: Tradeoffs in deployment strategies for YOLO:

left.text:
Runs large YOLO model
Maximum detection accuracy
Requires internet, introduces latency

right.text:
Runs tiny/nano YOLO model
Fastest real-time response
No internet required, lower accuracy

exercises.prompt: What does 'inference' mean in the context of deploying YOLO?

options.text: Running a trained model on new images to detect objects
Updating the weights of the neural network during training
Labeling raw images manually for training sets
Deleting the background of an image to save storage

exercises.explain: Correct! Inference is using a trained model to make predictions.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Running a model in real-time requires the model to process images faster than the frame rate of the video source.
exercises.explain: True. If a video is 30 FPS, the model must process each frame in under 33 milliseconds to maintain real-time tracking.

exercises.sentence: For deployment on low-power devices, we use smaller versions of YOLO like YOLO-___.
exercises.explain: YOLO-Tiny (or YOLO-Nano) is designed specifically for speed and memory efficiency on edge hardware.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a classic trade-off when using a smaller YOLO model?

options.text: Faster inference speed but slightly lower detection accuracy
Higher accuracy but slower training times
Larger model file size but less memory usage

exercises.explain: Correct! Smaller models use fewer layers, which increases speed but can miss very small or complex objects.

exercises.prompt: You are building a security system that runs on a battery-powered outdoor camera. Which setup is the most practical?

options.text: An optimized YOLO-Nano model running directly on the camera's low-power microcontroller to minimize latency and save battery.
A heavy ResNet-based Faster R-CNN model running on the camera.
Streaming 4K video continuously to a cloud server running a giant ensemble model.
Disabling object detection and running classification on raw pixel counts.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Edge models like YOLO-Nano are ideal for battery-powered, low-bandwidth smart devices. Phase 8: Modern Object Detection is complete. Next up: Image Segmentation.
